title: Scrambled eggs
subtitle: Slow-cooked eggs in butter, soft and just-set throughout.
excerpt: Eggs beaten and cooked slowly in butter over a low heat, stirred constantly until they form soft, barely-set curds. The French method. Takes eight minutes and produces something quite different from eggs cooked fast.
sourceNotes: Scrambled eggs cooked slowly in butter over low heat is the French tradition, documented in Escoffier and Larousse Gastronomique. The British tradition, by contrast, tends towards higher heat and looser results. The slow-cooked version produces a creamier, more cohesive texture and has become the standard in British restaurant and home cookery over the past thirty years.

content.text: Fast-scrambled eggs set firm and dry. Slow-scrambled eggs set soft and creamy, still barely liquid at the centre of each curd. The difference is entirely heat: low and patient, never over medium.

Three eggs per person is the right quantity. Two is too little; four produces too much to cook evenly in a small pan. The eggs should be at room temperature: cold eggs from the fridge take longer to set and can cook unevenly.
A small saucepan and a silicone spatula. A frying pan also works but a saucepan's higher sides make it easier to stir without spilling.

Crack {{eggs}} of eggs into the saucepan. Do not beat them first. Put the pan over a low heat and add {{unsalted-butter}} of butter. As the butter melts, begin stirring with a spatula, moving the eggs constantly across the base of the pan.
Cook slowly and pull off the heat

Keep the heat low and stir continuously for 6 to 8 minutes. The eggs will slowly form small, soft curds. When the eggs are three-quarters set but still look slightly underdone, pull the pan off the heat. The residual heat will finish them. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Serve immediately on toast with chives if using.

items.fix: Remove from heat the moment they look underdone. They continue cooking from residual heat. If dry, add a small knob of cold butter and stir: it will not reverse the texture but will add some richness back.
items.cause: Heat too high, or left on the heat too long.

items.fix: This cannot be reversed. The narrow window between underdone and weeping is why constant attention matters.
items.cause: Overcooked past the point where the proteins tighten and begin to expel liquid.

items.fix: Start with more butter and keep the heat lower. A non-stick pan also helps though it is not essential.
items.cause: Not enough butter, or heat too high.
items.symptom: Eggs are sticking to the pan

items.fix: Season with salt immediately before serving. Salt added too early draws moisture out of the eggs.
items.cause: Seasoned too late, or too little salt.

content.text: Scrambled eggs are one of the test dishes that experienced cooks use to assess a kitchen: they reveal immediately whether the cook understands heat control. The slow French method was codified by Escoffier but the principle is older, beurrer des oeufs brouillés appears in seventeenth-century French cookery. In British kitchens it competes with the faster, more casual version that produces looser, less uniform curds. Both are correct. The slow version rewards patience; the fast version rewards hunger.
